title: Vanilla Bean Marshmallow Fluff (4 Ingredients)
description: Light, fluffy homemade vanilla bean marshmallow fluff made with just 4 ingredients. Refined sugar free, gut friendly, and perfect for toast, s'mores, or baking.

hero.alt: Vanilla bean marshmallow fluff piped into a tall swirl on top of a glass jar on a marble surface with a white tile background

thumbnail.alt: Close-up of vanilla bean marshmallow fluff swirled into a peak on top of a glass jar

instructions.text: To your stand mixer add the gelatin and 1/2 cup water. Let it bloom for at least 5 minutes.
Slice the vanilla pod lengthwise and scrape out the seeds.
Transfer the vanilla seeds to a saucepan and add the remaining 1/2 cup water and maple syrup or honey.
Heat on low until hot but not boiling.
Switch the stand mixer on and slowly pour in the hot liquid.
Beat on high speed for 8–10 minutes until the mixture thickens and can loosely hold its shape — this is your fluff.
Transfer to a container and enjoy! It will set over time. To soften again, place the container in hot water or microwave in short bursts until it reaches your desired texture.

notes: Stop beating as soon as the fluff thickens and can loosely hold its shape — unlike marshmallows, you don't want it to fully set.
If the fluff thickens too much, gently warm it in the microwave in short bursts or place the bowl over warm water.
Ensure the gelatin is fully bloomed before adding the hot liquid.

story.subtitle: light, fluffy 4-ingredient refined sugar free treat
paragraphs: How dreamy is this 4-ingredient homemade vanilla bean marshmallow fluff? This recipe is especially nostalgic for me because I grew up eating marshmallow fluff and putting it on absolutely everything.

This version is a much healthier alternative, made with just four simple ingredients and refined sugar-free. It has the same soft, pillowy texture you remember, but with ingredients that feel a little more nourishing.
If you've been wanting to try making homemade marshmallows (like my 4-ingredient matcha marshmallows) but feel slightly intimidated, this marshmallow fluff is the perfect place to start.

One of the trickiest parts of making marshmallows is knowing exactly when to stop beating the mixture. Beat it too little and it won't hold its shape, but beat it too long and the mixture can become too stiff and start setting in the bowl before you can transfer it. The exact timing can vary depending on your mixer's speed and power, as well as the temperature of the sugar mixture.

Marshmallow fluff is a clever workaround for this. Since the goal is a soft, spreadable texture, you actually stop mixing before the mixture reaches the full marshmallow stage. As soon as the fluff thickens and can loosely hold its shape, it's ready. That makes this recipe much more forgiving and beginner-friendly, and a great stepping stone into the world of homemade marshmallows.
If your fluff thickens more than you'd like, you can easily loosen it again. Simply place the bowl over warm water or microwave it in short bursts until you reach your desired texture.

The flavour of this marshmallow fluff is elevated with real vanilla bean, which adds beautiful little flecks throughout the mixture and makes it feel extra special. It truly looks magical, and it's hard to believe it's made at home with just a handful of ingredients.

Gelatin is the real star of the show here. Not only does it create that classic marshmallow texture, but it also adds gut-soothing benefits and a small protein boost, making this sweet treat feel a little more balanced.
Enjoy this healthier marshmallow fluff spread on toast, dolloped onto desserts, or swirled into your baking. It's also perfect for making a healthier homemade version of s'mores.

faq.question: How do I store homemade marshmallow fluff?
faq.answer: Store marshmallow fluff in an airtight container in the refrigerator for up to 5 days. It will firm up as it chills, but you can easily soften it by gently warming it in the microwave for a few seconds or placing the container in warm water. Give it a stir after warming to restore the smooth, spreadable texture.

faq.question: What does it mean to bloom gelatin?
faq.answer: Blooming gelatin simply means allowing the gelatin powder to absorb liquid before it's heated. This step hydrates the gelatin so it dissolves smoothly and creates the soft, fluffy texture marshmallows are known for. Skipping this step can result in undissolved gelatin lumps in the final mixture, so make sure to bloom for at least 5 minutes.

faq.question: Can you make marshmallow fluff without corn syrup?
faq.answer: Yes, this marshmallow fluff recipe is made without corn syrup and so are my matcha marshmallows. Traditional marshmallow fluff recipes use corn syrup to prevent crystallisation and add body, but maple syrup or honey work beautifully as natural alternatives that keep this recipe refined sugar free.

faq.question: Can I use vanilla extract instead of vanilla bean?
faq.answer: Yes, vanilla extract works as a substitute. Use 1 to 1.5 teaspoons of good quality vanilla extract in place of the vanilla bean pod. The flavour will be slightly less complex and intense than real vanilla bean, and you won't get the beautiful little black flecks, but it still tastes absolutely delicious and is a much more affordable option.

faq.question: Why is my marshmallow fluff not thickening?
faq.answer: If the fluff isn't thickening, the most likely reason is that the hot liquid wasn't hot enough when added to the bloomed gelatin, or the mixer speed wasn't high enough. Make sure the maple syrup mixture is genuinely hot (steaming, not just warm) before slowly pouring it into the mixing bowl. Then beat on the highest speed your mixer allows and give it the full 8 to 10 minutes. The transformation from liquid to fluffy takes time and patience.

faq.question: Can I make marshmallow fluff without a stand mixer?
faq.answer: A stand mixer is the easiest way to make this fluff as the mixture needs sustained high-speed beating for 8 to 10 minutes. A handheld electric mixer also works but can be tiring to hold at that speed for that long. Using a whisk by hand is not suitable as the mixture sets quickly and needs more power than hand whisking can provide.

faq.question: Is this marshmallow fluff suitable for vegans?
faq.answer: Unfortunately, this recipe uses gelatin which is animal-derived, so it is not vegan. Agar-agar powder is sometimes suggested as a plant-based substitute, but it behaves very differently to gelatin — it sets firmer and is less stretchy, so it doesn't produce the same soft, pillowy marshmallow texture. If you need a vegan version, aquafaba (the liquid from a can of chickpeas) whipped with cream of tartar is a popular plant-based alternative to gelatin for marshmallow fluff.

faq.question: What can I use marshmallow fluff for?
faq.answer: So many things! Spread it on toast or waffles for a nostalgic, healthier treat. Swirl it into hot chocolate, oat milk lattes, or warm drinks for a luxurious frothy topping. Use it to make healthier s'mores with graham crackers and dark chocolate, or dollop it onto desserts for an impressive finish. It's also wonderful folded into smoothies or used as a topping for healthy ice cream. The flavour is neutral and sweet enough to work in almost any sweet context.

tips: Ensure the gelatin is properly bloomed for at least 5 minutes before adding the hot liquid.
Use real vanilla bean for the best flavour — the flecks make it look and taste extra special.
Serve it fresh for the best soft, spreadable texture.
Stop beating as soon as the fluff thickens and can loosely hold its shape — overmixing causes it to set in the bowl before you can transfer it to a container.

alternatives: 1 to 1.5 teaspoons vanilla extract (easier to find and more affordable — the fluff will still taste delicious)
1 teaspoon vanilla bean paste (gives the same black flecks and flavour intensity as a real vanilla bean)
Honey (not vegan, but gives a slightly floral, richer sweetness — works just as well)
Agave syrup (lighter, more neutral flavour and lower GI)
Rice syrup (gives a subtler sweetness with a slightly more neutral flavour)
Agar-agar powder can be used as a plant-based alternative, though the texture will be firmer and less pillowy than traditional marshmallow fluff
Always use unflavoured plain gelatin — bovine and porcine gelatin both work well
